ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for hot and sour vegetable soup; closest_parent 'vegetable soup food product' (FOODON_00002539) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC 'Soups and broths' is the closest defensible parent category; no specific hot-and-sour vegetable soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for hot and sour vegetable soup.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (cabbage, carrot, capsicum, mushroom, spring onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of hot and sour vegetable soup, approximately 300 g, as typically served at an Indian vegetarian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Sour Soup (vegetable, restaurant-style) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vegetable soup and component analysis
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-style hot and sour vegetable soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for vegetable soup (FDC ID 173170) and component-level data for vinegar, chilli sauce, and black pepper. The soup is predominantly water-based with low macronutrient density. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and chilli sauce. Values are consistent with published ranges for Indo-Chinese hot and sour vegetable soups in peer-reviewed dietary studies.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetable soups and mixed vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soup and low-GI mixed vegetable preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style hot and sour vegetable soup. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soups (GI range 25–48) and the low carbohydrate density of this dish. The acidic components (vinegar) are known to lower postprandial glycemic response per peer-reviewed evidence (Liljeberg & Bjorck, 1998, AJCN). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 4.8 × 3) / 100 = 5.0, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g bowl provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing about 42% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of 5 per standard serving, this soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the vinegar content may further attenuate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, no cholesterol, high vegetable content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The soup is low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option; however, the elevated sodium content warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup is an Indo-Chinese style soup popular in Indian vegetarian restaurants across Singapore and India. It is prepared by simmering finely chopped mixed vegetables — typically cabbage, carrot, capsicum, mushrooms, and spring onion — in a seasoned vegetable stock flavoured with chilli sauce, black pepper, vinegar, and soy sauce, then thickened with cornstarch to achieve a light, glossy consistency. The soup delivers a characteristic balance of sharp acidity from vinegar, pungent heat from black pepper and chilli, and umami depth from soy sauce. The texture is smooth and slightly viscous with tender vegetable pieces. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat, low-GI dish (approximately 28 kcal per 100 g, GI ~35) that is suitable for weight management and blood glucose control. The primary clinical concern is its moderate-to-high sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce and chilli sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is fully vegetarian, vegan, and halal. It is commonly served as a starter or light meal at Indian vegetarian restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is hot and sour vegetable soup healthy?
How many calories in hot and sour vegetable soup?

Is hot and sour vegetable soup high in sodium?

Is hot and sour soup vegan?
What is the GI of hot and sour soup?
Can I eat hot and sour soup if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-style hot and sour vegetable soup was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vegetable soup data and component-level analysis. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish is a well-characterised water-based vegetable soup with predictable macronutrient density, and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (vegetable soup, FDC ID 173170) with adjustments for soy sauce and chilli sauce sodium contribution. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based ingredients contain no industrial trans fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no fortified or animal-derived ingredients). GI estimated at 35 based on University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup analogues; vinegar content may further reduce postprandial glycaemic response per Liljeberg & Bjorck (1998). Sodium content is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The food_name has been normalised from 'Hot N Sour Vegetable Soup' to the canonical English form 'Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup'. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data for mixed vegetable soup.
